summary: The primary distinguishing feature is that macrocephaly does not align as a typical sequela for FAS/FASD; instead it suggests an alternative etiology. Holoprosencephaly, microcephaly, and thinning of the corpus callosum more commonly indicate serious neurodevelopmental issues often linked directly back to prenatal ethanol exposure but manifest differently from what would be expected under standard diagnostic criteria set forth by these conditions' definitions. Agent2 concludes based primarily upon differential diagnosis between listed craniofacial/brain malformations associated specifically within context provided regarding FAS vs those less likely seen alongside this condition such as isolated macrosomia without accompanying neurological deficits typically observed across broader range of affected individuals diagnosed clinically via established guidelines used internationally today when evaluating suspected cases involving maternal substance use during pregnancy periods critical early gestation stages impacting organogenesis processes most profoundly at risk then leading towards formation abnormalities later life presenting symptoms observable through physical exams combined radiological imaging studies confirming specific anatomical changes indicative respective disorders discussed here including both congenital heart disease which may also occur albeit rarely among subset patients meeting strict inclusion/exclusion criteria necessary diagnosing any one particular case definitively requiring multidisciplinary team approach ensuring comprehensive care plan addressing all aspects patient needs throughout course illness
